subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 16. In this unit you move beyond simply collecting numbers and drawing graphs. You will learn to interpret data – to say what it actually means – and to discuss results the way a real statistician does: carefully, honestly, and with a critical eye. Every day, people around you are handed statistics. A shopkeeper in Mogadishu hears that prices rose “on average” last month. A football fan reads that one striker “scores more consistently” than another. A news report claims that “9 out of 10 people prefer” a certain brand. Behind every one of those claims sits data that someone chose to summarise in a particular way. Your job this unit is to look behind the claim.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Lower Secondary Mathematics 0862 — Stage 8 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Calculate and interpret the mean, median and mode, and choose the most suitable average for a data set.
Measure the spread of data using the range and the interquartile range (IQR).
Compare two distributions by referring to an average and a measure of spread, in a written sentence.
Draw and interpret a scatter graph, and describe the correlation as positive, negative or none, and as strong or weak.
Draw a sensible line of best fit and use it to make predictions by interpolation and extrapolation.
Explain the difference between correlation and causation, and identify confounding factors.
Recognise misleading graphs, biased samples and unfair averages, and critique statistical claims.
Write a clear, evidence-based conclusion that answers a question and states its own limitations.

concepts.title: Averages – Choosing the Right One
concepts.explanation: An average is a single number that stands in for a whole set of values – a typical value. There are three averages you must know, and the skill in Year 8 is not just calculating them but choosing the one that describes the data most fairly. The mean is the total of all the values divided by how many values there are. Suppose seven students at Nuur Academy score 78, 82, 85, 79, 91, 88, 76 on a test. The mean is (78 + 82 + 85 + 79 + 91 + 88 + 76) ÷ 7 = 579 ÷ 7 = 82.7. The mean uses every single value, which is its…
concepts.example: The median is the middle value once the data is placed in order. Ordering the same scores gives 76, 78, 79, 82, 85, 88, 91, so the median is 82. When there is an even number of values, the median is the mean of the two…

concepts.title: Spread – Range and the Interquartile Range
concepts.explanation: An average tells you where the *centre* of the data sits, but it says nothing about how *spread out* the values are. Two footballers might both average 12 goals a season, yet one scores steadily each year while the other swings wildly between 2 and 25. To capture this, you need a measure of spread. The simplest measure of spread is the range: the highest value minus the lowest. For the scores 76 to 91, the range is 91 − 76 = 15. The range is quick to find, but it has a serious flaw: it depends only on the two most…
concepts.example: The interquartile range (IQR) fixes this. First you find the quartiles, which split the ordered data into four equal parts. Q1 (the lower quartile) has one quarter of the data below it; Q2 is the median; Q3 (the upper…

concepts.title: Comparing Two Distributions
concepts.explanation: A distribution is the whole picture of how a set of values is spread out. One of the most important skills in this unit is comparing two distributions – two classes, two players, two markets – and saying which is ‘better’ or how they differ. The golden rule is this: always compare an average AND a measure of spread, and always write your comparison in context. One number is never enough. Why both? Imagine two Year 8 classes sit the same maths test. Class A has a median of 62 and an IQR of 8. Class B has a median…
concepts.example: Now the other case: same spread, different centre. Suppose Hassan and Yusuf both play in the school football team. Over ten matches Hassan’s pass-completion has a median of 70% with an IQR of 6, and Yusuf’s has a median…

concepts.title: Scatter Graphs and Correlation
concepts.explanation: So far we have looked at one quantity at a time. A scatter graph lets you explore whether two quantities are related. You collect pairs of measurements – for each person, or day, or animal, you record two things – and plot each pair as a single point, with one variable on the x-axis and the other on the y-axis. For example, suppose you record the height and shoe size of six people: heights 155, 160, 165, 170, 175, 180 cm and shoe sizes 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11. Plotting the points (155, 6), (160, 7), (165, 8) and so on,…
concepts.example: The pattern the points make is called correlation. There are three basic kinds:

concepts.title: Correlation Is Not Causation
concepts.explanation: This is one of the most important ideas in all of statistics, and one of the easiest to get wrong. Just because two things are correlated – they rise and fall together – does not mean that one causes the other. A relationship in the data is a clue, not a proof. Here is the classic example. Across the year, ice-cream sales and cases of sunburn both go up and down together – a strong positive correlation. But ice cream does not cause sunburn, and sunburn does not cause people to buy ice cream. A third factor – hot,…
concepts.example: Consider a real-feeling case. A study finds that children who eat breakfast tend to score higher on tests – a positive correlation. It is tempting to conclude ‘breakfast causes higher marks’. But think of the…

concepts.title: Misleading Statistics and Unfair Samples
concepts.explanation: Numbers can be used to inform you – or to fool you. Part of interpreting results is learning the common tricks, so you can spot them and refuse to be misled. Misleading scales. The most common trick is a graph whose y-axis does not start at zero. Suppose a company’s sales rose from £100 million to £110 million – a modest 10% rise. If the y-axis starts at £95 million, the bar for £110 looks about three times taller than the bar for £100, making a small change appear dramatic. Always check where the axis starts…
concepts.example: Cherry-picking. Someone shows only the time period that flatters their case – ‘sales have risen every month since June’ – while hiding that sales fell sharply from January to June. Choosing a convenient start point, or…

methods.title: How to find the interquartile range
methods.example: Find the mean, median, mode and range of: 15, 18, 22, 18, 25, 30, 18, 27.
steps: Put all the data in order from smallest to largest.
Find the median (Q2) – the middle value.
Find Q1, the median of the lower half, and Q3, the median of the upper half.
Calculate IQR = Q3 − Q1.

methods.title: How to compare two distributions
methods.example: Find the mean, median, mode and range of: 15, 18, 22, 18, 25, 30, 18, 27.
steps: Find an average (usually the median) for each set.
Find a measure of spread (usually the IQR) for each set.
Check the how to compare two distributions result carefully.

methods.title: Compare the spreads: which is more consistent, and what does that mean?
steps: Write one sentence covering both, in context.
Check the compare the spreads: which is more consistent, and what does that mean? result carefully.
Check the compare the spreads: which is more consistent, and what does that mean? result carefully.

methods.title: How to use a scatter graph
methods.example: Class A: median 64, IQR 6. Class B: median 58, IQR 15. Compare the two classes’ test performance.
steps: Plot each pair of values as a point.
Describe the correlation: direction (positive/negative/none) and strength (strong/weak).
Draw a line of best fit balancing points above and below.
Predict by interpolation (inside the data – reliable) or note extrapolation (outside – risky).

methods.title: Describing correlation
methods.example: On a scatter graph of ‘hours of revision’ against ‘test score’, the points rise from bottom-left to top-right and lie fairly close to a straight line. Describe the correlation and what it means.
steps: Solution: The points slope upward and lie close to a line, so there is a strong positive correlation between revision time and test score.
In context: students who revised for longer tended to score higher marks.
(Remember: this is a link, not proof that revision alone causes the higher score.)

methods.title: Interpolation vs extrapolation
methods.example: Farah’s scatter graph covers revision times from 1 to 5 hours. She uses her line of best fit to predict the score for (a) 3 hours and (b) 12 hours. Which prediction is reliable?
steps: Solution: Prediction (a) at 3 hours is inside the data range (1 to 5 hours), so it is interpolation and is reliable.
Prediction (b) at 12 hours is outside the range, so it is extrapolation – unreliable, because there is no evidence the trend continues that far, and the score cannot exceed 100% anyway.
Check the interpolation vs extrapolation result carefully.

workedExamples.title: Mean, median, mode and range
workedExamples.prompt: Find the mean, median, mode and range of: 15, 18, 22, 18, 25, 30, 18, 27.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: There are 8 values. Mean = (15 + 18 + 22 + 18 + 25 + 30 + 18 + 27) ÷ 8 = 173 ÷ 8 = 21.625. To find the median, order them: 15, 18, 18, 18, 22, 25, 27, 30. With 8 values the median is the mean of the 4th and 5th: (18 + 22) ÷ 2 = 20. The mode is 18 (it appears three times). The range is 30 − 15 = 15. Worked Example 2: Which average is fairest? A small workshop pays five workers 200, 220, 210, 230 and 900 shillings a day. Which average best describes a typical wage? Solution: The mean is (200 + 220 + 210 +…

workedExamples.title: Interquartile range
workedExamples.solution: Solution: There are 11 values in order. The median (Q2) is the 6th value, 25. The lower half (below the median) is 12, 15, 18, 20, 22, so Q1 = 18. The upper half is 28, 30, 35, 38, 42, so Q3 = 35. Therefore IQR = 35 − 18 = 17. This is smaller than the range (30), showing the middle half of the data is more tightly packed than the extremes suggest.

workedExamples.title: Comparing two distributions
workedExamples.prompt: Class A: median 64, IQR 6. Class B: median 58, IQR 15. Compare the two classes’ test performance.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: ‘Class A has a higher median (64 vs 58), so Class A performed better on average. Class A also has a smaller IQR (6 vs 15), so Class A’s marks are more consistent, while Class B’s marks are much more spread out.’ A full comparison names the average and the spread, both in context – that is what earns full marks.

workedExamples.title: Describing correlation
workedExamples.prompt: On a scatter graph of ‘hours of revision’ against ‘test score’, the points rise from bottom-left to top-right and lie fairly close to a straight line. Describe the correlation and what it means.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The points slope upward and lie close to a line, so there is a strong positive correlation between revision time and test score. In context: students who revised for longer tended to score higher marks. (Remember: this is a link, not proof that revision alone causes the higher score.)

workedExamples.title: Interpolation vs extrapolation
workedExamples.prompt: Farah’s scatter graph covers revision times from 1 to 5 hours. She uses her line of best fit to predict the score for (a) 3 hours and (b) 12 hours. Which prediction is reliable?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: Prediction (a) at 3 hours is inside the data range (1 to 5 hours), so it is interpolation and is reliable. Prediction (b) at 12 hours is outside the range, so it is extrapolation – unreliable, because there is no evidence the trend continues that far, and the score cannot exceed 100% anyway.

workedExamples.title: Correlation vs causation
workedExamples.prompt: A newspaper reports: ‘Towns with more mobile phones have more cases of illness – phones are making people sick.’ Critique this conclusion.
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The conclusion mistakes correlation for causation. A likely confounding variable is population size: bigger towns have more of *everything* – more phones and more illness cases – without one causing the other. A fair comparison would use rates (cases per 1000 people), not raw totals. So the newspaper’s claim is not supported by the evidence given.

workedExamples.title: Critiquing a claim
workedExamples.prompt: ‘A survey outside the Hargeisa sports stadium found that 95% of people exercise daily, so most people in the city are very active.’ What is wrong with this conclusion?
workedExamples.solution: Solution: The sample is biased. People outside a sports stadium are far more likely to exercise than the general public, so this is selection bias – the sample is not representative of the whole city. The 95% figure describes stadium-goers, not everyone. A fair study would sample people from many different places across the city, chosen at random.

workedExamples.title: Practice 9

workedExamples.prompt: Class R: median 50, IQR 4. Class S: median 50, IQR 18. Write a full sentence comparing them.
workedExamples.solution: , so Class Q performed better on average.

activities.title: Your Own Data Investigation
activities.materials: paper, a pencil, and about 15 people to ask (family, friends, classmates).
steps: Choose a question with a number answer, e.g. ‘How many minutes do you walk to the masjid or school?’
Collect the data from at least 15 people and write it in a list.
Calculate the mean, median, mode and range.
Find Q1, Q3 and the IQR. Which measure of spread do you trust more, and why?
Write two sentences: one describing a typical value, one describing the spread – both in context.

activities.title: The Scatter-Graph Hunt
activities.materials: paper, ruler, and pairs of measurements you can collect.
steps: Pick two things you think might be related, e.g. a person’s height and their arm span, or hours slept and mood score out of 10.
Measure both for at least 10 people and record the pairs.
Plot the pairs as points on a scatter graph.
Describe the correlation (type and strength) and draw a sensible line of best fit.
Use your line to predict one value by interpolation – then discuss whether extrapolating far beyond your data would be safe.

activities.title: Spot the Misleading Graph (Media Game)
activities.materials: newspapers, adverts, or online articles with graphs and statistics.
steps: Collect three graphs or statistical claims from any source.
For each, check: does the y-axis start at zero? Which average is quoted? How big was the sample?
Decide whether each is fair or misleading, and explain how you could fix a misleading one.
Make a ‘Statistics Hall of Fame and Shame’ poster with your best and worst examples.
This trains the single most useful habit in the unit: never believing a graph until you have read its scale.

activities.title: The Correlation-or-Cause Debate
activities.materials: a partner or family member to debate with.
steps: Write five ‘A goes with B’ statements, e.g. ‘towns with more phones have more illness’.
For each, one person argues ‘A causes B’ and the other must find a confounding variable.
Award a point every time someone names a believable hidden third factor.
Discuss: which of your five statements might actually be causal, and how could you test it fairly?

activities.title: Compare Two Distributions (Group Task)
activities.materials: your data from Activity 1 and a classmate’s data on the same question.
steps: Put both data sets side by side. Calculate the median and IQR for each.
Write a full comparison sentence using both an average and a spread, in context.
If you can, sketch two box plots on the same scale and check they agree with your sentence.
Discuss whether your two samples are big enough and fair enough to draw any real conclusion.

activities.title: Design a Fair Survey (Investigation)
steps: Choose a question people disagree about, e.g. ‘Should the school day start later?’
Write a leading version of the question, then rewrite it as a neutral one.
Decide who you would ask and how, to avoid selection bias – explain your sampling choice.
Predict how a biased sample might change your result, then (if you can) test it on two different groups.

After your activities, talk or write about: Which activity best showed you why spread matters as much as the average? When was it hardest to tell correlation from cause? Which misleading trick would fool most people you know? Share your thoughts with your teacher at your next session.

rules.title: A distribution is the whole picture of
rules.text: A distribution is the whole picture of how a set of values is spread out. One of the most important skills in this unit is comparing two distributions – two classes, two players, two markets – and saying which is ‘better’ or how they differ. The golden rule is this: always compare an average AND a measure of spread, and always write your comparison in context. One number is never enough.

terms: The three averages: total÷n / middle / most frequent
Values splitting ordered data into quarters
Q3 − Q1; spread of the middle 50%
How a set of values is spread out
A straight line through the trend of a scatter graph
Predicting inside / outside the data range
One thing actually making another happen
A hidden factor affecting both variables
A systematic error that skews results one way
A value far from the rest of the data
commonMistakes: Give an average AND a spread, in context
Predict only near the measured range

realProblems.prompt: The daily numbers of customers at Hassan’s Eastleigh shop over a week were 40, 44, 41, 43, 120, 42, 45. Find the mean and median. Explain which is the fairer typical value and why the other is misleading.
realProblems.answer: 1) Mean = 475÷7 ≈ 67.9; ordered 40,41,42,43,44,45,120, median = 43. The median (43) is fairer; the mean is misleading because the outlier 120 (an unusually busy day) pulls it far above every other day.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.
realProblems.errorFeedback: 1) Mean = 475÷7 ≈ 67.9; ordered 40,41,42,43,44,45,120, median = 43. The median (43) is fairer; the mean is misleading because the outlier 120 (an unusually busy day) pulls it far above every other day.

realProblems.prompt: A drinks company advertises: ‘70% of people preferred our juice’. The survey was 20 people at the company’s own stall. Write a short paragraph critiquing this claim, mentioning sample size and bias.
realProblems.answer: is more variable; before choosing, you might want the medians, recent form, and the strength of the opposition faced.
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

realProblems.prompt: Temperature (°C) and ice-cream sales at a Kismaayo beach stall over six days: (24, 30), (26, 38), (28, 45), (30, 52), (32, 60), (34, 66). Describe the correlation, and use the trend to estimate sales at 29°C (interpolation). Then explain why estimating sales at 45°C would be unreliable.
realProblems.answer: Example: ‘There is a moderate positive correlation, so players who train more tend to score more goals. However, this is not proof that training causes more goals – a confounding factor such as natural ability or match time could affect both.’
realProblems.hint: Identify the key mathematical idea before calculating or explaining.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A report says: ‘Villages with more storks have more babies born, so storks bring babies.’ Identify the confounding variable and explain the real reason for the correlation.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: 1) The confounding variable is town/village size: larger settlements have more buildings (more stork nests) and more people (more babies). Storks do not cause births; population size drives both.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: A company’s sales bar chart has a y-axis starting at 980 instead of 0, making a rise from 1000 to 1010 look enormous. Explain two things wrong with this and how to fix them.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Problems: the truncated y-axis exaggerates a tiny 1% rise, and a reader is misled into seeing a dramatic increase. Fix: start the axis at 0, or clearly mark an axis break, and label the true values.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Two goalkeepers each concede a mean of 1 goal per match. Keeper A has an IQR of 0; Keeper B has an IQR of 3. Which would you sign, and why? Justify your choice – there is no single correct answer.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Either answer is acceptable with justification: Keeper A (IQR

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Amina surveys people leaving the library and concludes ‘most young people love reading’. Explain the bias in her sample and describe a fairer method.
Explain the key idea in Correlation Is Not Causation.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: This is one of the most important ideas in all of statistics, and one of the easiest to get wrong. Just because two things are correlated – they rise and fall together – does not mean that one causes the other. A relationship in the data is a clue, not a proof. Here is the classic example. Across the year, ice-cream sales and cases of sunburn both go up and down together – a strong positive correlation. But ice cream does not cause sunburn, and sunburn does not cause people to buy ice cream. A third factor – hot,…

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Explain the key idea in Misleading Statistics and Unfair Samples.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Numbers can be used to inform you – or to fool you. Part of interpreting results is learning the common tricks, so you can spot them and refuse to be misled. Misleading scales. The most common trick is a graph whose y-axis does not start at zero. Suppose a company’s sales rose from £100 million to £110 million – a modest 10% rise. If the y-axis starts at £95 million, the bar for £110 looks about three times taller than the bar for £100, making a small change appear dramatic. Always check where the axis starts…

selfAssessment: I can calculate and interpret the mean, median and mode, and choose the most suitable average for a data set.
I can measure the spread of data using the range and the interquartile range (IQR).
I can compare two distributions by referring to an average and a measure of spread, in a written sentence.
I can draw and interpret a scatter graph, and describe the correlation as positive, negative or none, and as strong or weak.
I can draw a sensible line of best fit and use it to make predictions by interpolation and extrapolation.
I can explain the difference between correlation and causation, and identify confounding factors.
I can recognise misleading graphs, biased samples and unfair averages, and critique statistical claims.
I can write a clear, evidence-based conclusion that answers a question and states its own limitations.
